content.problemStatement:
You are given a binary string SSS of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on it:


Choose an index iii (1≤i<N1 \leq i \lt N1≤i<N) such that Si=Si+1S_i = S_{i+1}Si​=Si+1​, and flip both SiS_iSi​ and Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1​.

Flipping SiS_iSi​ means it becomes 111 if it was originally 000, and vice versa.
For example, if S=0101100S = \texttt{0101100}S=0101100, performing the operation with i=4i = 4i=4 would result in S=01000‾00S = \texttt{010\underline{00}00}S=01000​00 and performing it with i=6i = 6i=6 would result in S=0101111‾S = \texttt{01011\underline{11}}S=0101111​.

By performing this operation several (possibly, zero) times, is it possible to make SSS contain only a single type of character (that is, SSS should contain either all zeros or all ones)?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of SSS.
The second line of each test case contains a binary string SSS.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `Yes` if it is possible for SSS to contain only zeros or only ones, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2*10^5
S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the binary string. The second line contains a binary string S of length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if it is possible to make the string consist of only one character using the allowed operations, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation: The condition reduces to: if the count of '0's is even or the count of '1's is even, answer Yes; otherwise No.
First string already uniform → Yes. Second string has no equal adjacent pair and counts are both odd → No.
Number of zeros = 1 (odd), number of ones = 2 (even) → Yes.

solution.approach: Let cnt be the number of '1's in S. Each operation flips two equal adjacent bits, which changes cnt by ±2, therefore the parity of cnt never changes. To obtain a string of all zeros we need cnt = 0 (even), and to obtain all ones we need cnt = N (parity of N). Hence the transformation is possible iff either cnt is even (we can reach all zeros) or cnt and N have the same parity (we can reach all ones). This condition simplifies to: answer is "Yes" if N is odd or cnt is even; otherwise "No". The algorithm just counts ones and checks the condition for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(total length of all strings) which is ≤ 2·10^5.
